hub_subtitle: Business English para People & Culture, comunicação executiva e a liderança da divisão do Canadá

lesson.menu_title: The Coaching 1:1 -- Developing People, Not Managing Them
lesson.menu_desc: Running a developmental one-on-one with a talented mentee who is losing confidence and close to quitting: listen before you advise, reframe 'I am failing' into 'I am on a learning curve', find the growth edge, and give the guidance that develops instead of the order that shrinks -- developmental feedback, growth edge, coachable moment, active listening, reframing, sounding board, stretch assignment, coaching mindset, boundary setting, career aspirations, to empower, to check in, to play to someone's strengths, to bite the bullet, to throw in the towel. Structure: advanced modals -- would rather / had better / may as well (I would rather we solved the root cause / you had better talk to your manager before the deadline slips / if you are redoing it anyway, you may as well show what you learned). -- 36 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 14 -- The Coaching 1:1
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Danielle Moreira | Lesson 14 -- The Coaching 1:1
lesson.grammar_point: advanced modals: would rather / had better / may as well

listenings.text: Hi Danielle, it is Owen. I am recording this the night before our one-on-one because I know if I wait until we are on the call I will just say everything is fine, and it is not. I have missed two deadlines now, my replies have gotten shorter, and honestly I have been quiet because I did not want you to see me struggling. I keep thinking maybe I am just not cut out for this. Everyone else seems to move faster, and I am drowning in a stack of tasks that I am genuinely bad at, and instead of asking for help I have been staying late and getting further behind. I know I should have flagged this weeks ago. I did not, and now it feels too big to bring up, which is exactly why I am bringing it up in a voice note where you cannot see my face. Part of me wants to just throw in the towel and ask for a transfer, or leave. But another part of me does not want to quit the one thing I actually care about getting good at. So I do not really know what I am asking for. Maybe just, do not fix it for me. I have had managers who fixed everything and I learned nothing. I would rather come out of this able to handle the next one myself. Anyway. See you tomorrow. Thanks for listening to the long version.

Danielle, it is Ruth, your coach. You forwarded me Owen's voice note and asked how to run the one-on-one, and I want to slow you down before you walk in, because your instinct is going to fight you the whole time. You are fast, you are good at fixing things, and the fastest, kindest-feeling thing to do will be to take the hard tasks off his plate and hand him a plan. Do not. If you solve it, you rob him of the one thing he actually asked you for, which is to come out of this able to handle the next one himself. So here is the shift. Walk in with a coaching mindset, which just means you lead with questions, not answers. Open with something real, help me understand what got in the way, and then do the hardest thing for someone like you, which is nothing. Be quiet. Let the silence sit until he says the thing under the thing. That is active listening, and it is where the whole conversation turns. When you do guide, guide, do not command. There is a beautiful set of modals for exactly this. I would rather we solved the root cause than patch it, that owns your preference without an order. You had better talk to your manager before the next deadline, that lands a warning without you raising your voice. And if he is redoing the report anyway, he may as well use it to show what he learned. Warm, but directive. That is the whole register. One more thing. His growth edge is not effort, he has plenty. It is asking for help before things slip. Name that, gently, and give him a stretch assignment that plays to what he is good at. You are not there to rescue Owen. You are there to empower him. Go do the thing you are actually great at, which is seeing people clearly, and for once, keep your solutions in your pocket.

paras: Owen was a talented junior coordinator, and for two weeks he had gone quiet. Deadlines slipped, his replies got shorter, and the night before his one-on-one he left Danielle a voice note that all but said he was ready to throw in the towel. The easy move -- the one that would have felt kind and fast -- was to take the hard tasks off his plate and hand him a plan. Danielle did the opposite. She walked in with a coaching mindset, which is a fancy way of saying she led with a question instead of a verdict, and then did the single hardest thing for a fast, capable leader: she was quiet. Active listening is not waiting for your turn to talk; it is letting the silence sit until the person says the thing under the thing.

What Owen said, once the silence did its work, was not that he was lazy. He was drowning in tasks that did not play to his strengths, and he had been too proud to check in before things slipped. So Danielle reframed the story. "You are not failing," she told him. "You are on the steep part of the learning curve." That single move -- reframing -- did more than any instruction could have, because it changed what the problem was. And only then did she guide, carefully choosing the register of a leader who advises without commanding: "I would rather we solved the root cause together than patch the same thing next month. And honestly, you had better talk to your manager before the next deadline slips -- it is far easier now than later." When Owen admitted he would have to redo an entire report regardless, she smiled. "If you are redoing it anyway, you may as well use it to show me exactly what you have learned."

None of this was a scorecard. It was developmental feedback -- input aimed at growth, not a verdict on the past -- and it landed because she had earned the right to give it by listening first. She stayed a sounding board rather than a judge, so Owen could think out loud without defending himself. She named his real growth edge, which was not effort but asking for help early, and she set a stretch assignment just beyond his comfort: running the next round of interviews, work that pointed straight at his career aspirations. A task a size too big is exactly where growth happens, provided the person is not left alone in it.

The point of the hour was never to rescue Owen; it was to empower him to solve the next problem without her. She modelled boundary setting, too, telling him plainly what she could and could not take off his plate, so the help stayed honest and the ownership stayed his. He had walked in ready to quit and walked out with one clear task and a reason to stay. Sometimes leadership means you bite the bullet and have the difficult conversation. More often, as Danielle's own coach likes to remind her, it means you ask the right question and then keep your solutions in your pocket long enough to hear the answer.
reading.source: Field note, People & Culture — Danielle's mentoring log, Maple Bear

gist.prompt: Read once for the main idea. Which sentence is the argument of the whole text?
choices: A leader should solve a struggling mentee's problems quickly and take the hard tasks off their plate.
Because the goal of a developmental 1:1 is to empower, not to rescue, the leader listens and reframes first, then guides with warm but directive language instead of giving orders.
Developmental feedback works best when it is delivered as a detailed scorecard of past mistakes.
The fastest way to keep a mentee who wants to quit is to give them fewer responsibilities.

items: When Owen went quiet, Danielle's first move was to take the hard tasks off his plate and hand him a plan.
She did the opposite: she led with a question, listened, and guided him -- rather than solving it for him -- so he could handle the next one himself.
Active listening in the text means letting the silence sit until the person says the real problem.
It is not waiting for your turn to talk; it is staying quiet until Owen said the thing under the thing.
Danielle reframed Owen's story from 'I am failing' to 'I am on the steep part of the learning curve'.
That reframing changed what the problem was, and did more than any instruction could have.
Danielle gave Owen a scorecard of everything he had done wrong.
She gave developmental feedback -- input aimed at growth, not a verdict on the past -- and stayed a sounding board, not a judge.
The purpose of the 1:1 was to rescue Owen by solving the problem for him.
The purpose was to empower him to solve the next problem without her -- she even set boundaries so the ownership stayed his.

items.situation: "A mentee says: 'I think I'm just not good at this. Maybe I should quit.' Respond so you open the door instead of closing it."
tips: Help me understand what got in the way. You are not failing -- you are on the steep part of the learning curve, and I would rather grow you than replace you. What would it look like if the next month went differently?
reframe the story first, then use 'would rather' to state your preference -- own it without ordering him to stay

items.situation: "Your mentee keeps missing deadlines and only tells you afterwards. Give urgent advice that carries a warning, without raising your voice."
tips: You had better talk to your manager before the next deadline slips, not after -- it is far easier now. And you had better not wait until it is a crisis to check in with me.
'had better' is strong advice with a warning; keep it warm by pairing it with why -- it is easier now

items.situation: "Owen admits he'll have to redo a whole report anyway and is dreading it. Turn the sunk cost into growth."
tips: If you are redoing it regardless, you may as well use it to show me exactly what you have learned. Same work, better payoff -- let us make the redo the stretch assignment, not a punishment.
'may as well' makes the decision feel easy; frame the redo as a stretch assignment that plays to his strengths

items.situation: "A mentee is drowning in tasks that don't suit them. What is the coaching move -- in one or two sentences?"
tips: Let us play to your strengths. I would rather move you onto the interviews, where you are genuinely strong, and empower you to run them, than keep you buried in spreadsheets you dread.
name the mismatch, then 'would rather' + a stretch assignment that fits their strengths and their career aspirations

items.situation: "Your mentee opens up and is close to giving up. Name the real growth edge without it sounding like criticism."
tips: What I am noticing is that your growth edge is not effort -- you have plenty. It is asking for help before things slip. I would rather you didn't carry it alone; that is what I am here for.
developmental feedback about the pattern, not the person; 'would rather you didn't' (past form, different subject)

items.situation: "Summarize, for your own coach, how you kept a mentee who wanted to quit."
tips: I led with a question, not a verdict, and I listened until he named the real problem. I reframed it as a learning curve, named his growth edge, and guided him -- I would rather grow him than replace him. I gave him a stretch assignment and empowered him to run it. He walked out with a reason to stay.
the close: listen, reframe, guide with the modals, empower -- rescue was never the point
